content.problemStatement:
Chef has the NNN numbers 1,2,3,…,N1, 2, 3, \dots, N1,2,3,…,N. He wants to give exactly KKK of these numbers to his friend and keep the rest with him.

He can choose any KKK numbers such that the GCD of any number from Chef's set and any number from his friend's set is equal to 111.

Formally, suppose Chef gives the set of numbers AAA to his friend and keeps BBB with himself (where ∣A∣=K|A| = K∣A∣=K and ∣B∣=N−K|B| = N - K∣B∣=N−K). Then AAA and BBB must satisfy

gcd⁡(a,b)=1  ∀a∈A,b∈B\gcd(a, b) = 1 \ \ \forall a\in A, b\in Bgcd(a,b)=1  ∀a∈A,b∈BChef needs your help in choosing these KKK numbers. Please find any valid set of KKK numbers that will satisfy the condition, or tell him that no such set exists.


Input Format

The first line of input contains a single integer TTT denoting the number of test cases. The description of TTT test cases follows.
The first and only line of each test case contains two space-separated integers NNN and KKK.


Output Format
For each test case first output a single line containing "YES" (without quotes) if a set of size KKK satisfying Chef's condition exists; and "NO" if no such set exists. This line is not case-sensitive so "YeS", "nO", etc. are also acceptable.


Next, if the answer is "YES", print another line containing KKK distinct space-separated integers from 111 to NNN denoting the numbers which Chef will give to his friend. The integers can be printed in any order.


If there are multiple solutions, you may print any of them.
constraints: Sum of N over all test cases ≤ 5·10^5
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains two integers N and K (2 ≤ N ≤ 10^5, 1 ≤ K ≤ N‑1).
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a line with "YES" if a suitable set of K numbers exists, otherwise output "NO". If the answer is "YES", on the next line output K distinct integers from 1 to N that form the required set (any order).

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: Chef can give [3][3][3] to his friend and keep [1,2,4][1, 2, 4][1,2,4] for himself. 333 is coprime with 1,21, 21,2 and 444 so the condition is satisfied. Another possible solution is Chef giving [1][1][1] to his friend.
Test case 222: Chef can give [2,4][2, 4][2,4] and keep [1,3][1, 3][1,3] (or vice versa). It can be seen that gcd⁡(2,1)=1\gcd(2, 1) = 1gcd(2,1)=1, gcd⁡(2,3)=1\gcd(2, 3) = 1gcd(2,3)=1, gcd⁡(4,1)=1\gcd(4, 1) = 1gcd(4,1)=1, gcd⁡(4,3)=1\gcd(4, 3) = 1gcd(4,3)=1 and so the condition is satisfied.
Test case 333: There is no set of 3 numbers that can satisfy the given condition.

solution.approach:
For any prime p, all numbers that are multiples of p must belong to the same side, otherwise a pair with gcd ≥ p would appear. Therefore all primes that appear together in some number must be placed on the same side. Build a graph whose vertices are primes ≤ N and connect two primes if they both divide some integer ≤ N. The connected components of this graph represent groups of primes that must stay together. All numbers that have at least one prime from a component belong to that component. The number 1 has no prime factor and can be placed arbitrarily.

Thus the problem becomes: we have several groups, each group i contains cnt[i] numbers (the numbers belonging to the i‑th prime component) and one extra group of size 1 for the number 1. We have to choose some groups completely so that the total size equals K. This is a classic subset‑sum problem. Because the total N over all test cases is only 5·10^5, a bitset DP (O(N·#components/word‑size)) is fast enough.

Algorithm per test case:
1. Sieve smallest prime factor (SPF) up to N.
2. Initialise a DSU for all primes ≤ N.
3. For each integer x from 2 to N, factorise it using SPF, obtain its distinct prime factors. Union all those primes in the DSU – this merges primes that appear together.
4. After processing all numbers, for each integer x (2…N) take any of its prime factors, find the DSU root of that prime and increase the counter of that component. Store the actual numbers belonging to each component for later reconstruction.
5. Create a list `sizes` containing the counters of all distinct components and also add an extra size 1 for the number 1.
6. Run a bitset DP: dp[0]=1; for each size s in `sizes` do dp |= dp << s. Keep a predecessor array to be able to reconstruct which components were taken.
7. If dp[K] is false → output "NO".
8. Otherwise reconstruct the chosen components, collect all numbers belonging to those components (and possibly the number 1) and output them.

Complexities: The sieve and factorisation are O(N log log N). DSU operations are almost O(N). The DP works on a bitset of length N+1, so total time per test case is O(N / 64 * #components) ≤ O(N·log N / 64) which is well within limits. Memory usage is O(N) for the sieve, DSU, component lists and the DP bitset.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N log log N + N·α(N) + (N/64)·C) per test case, where C is the number of prime components (C ≤ number of primes ≤ N). In practice ≤ O(N·log N / 64).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) – for SPF array, DSU, component containers and the DP bitset.
